data.title: Total Return Breakouts: 3 Tariff Trades As China, Value, And Financial Stocks Move Higher
data.description:
Introduction

The unpredictability of noise traders' beliefs creates a risk in the price of the asset that deters rational arbitragers from aggressively betting against them. As a result, prices can diverge significantly from fundamental values even in the absence of fundamental risk.

~ DeLong J. B. et al. (1990).

I have spent the last 35 years trading, researching, and constructing algorithms to identify and leverage the value across fundamental, technical, and behavioral finance models. Of the ten portfolio models designed for optimal portfolio mixes for members to beat the market at Value & Momentum Breakouts, eight come from enhancing well-tested anomaly research in published financial journals.

If you believe like I do, that there is much more to stock trading than just buy, hold, and hope that every future event works out perfectly as promised for your stock – then these trading models are for you. This article highlights some of our recent top trades from three very different portfolio models, ranging from aggressive short-term weekly breakouts to long-term growth and value portfolios with high dividends.

Three Trades from Different Portfolio Models

In this article, I provide another sampling of how we use the optimal portfolio models to identify long-term continued strength, short-term aggressive breakout stocks, and even apply timing models to long-term value stocks for the best results. As I repeatedly tell our investment community, it is important to not only diversify your stock selections, but it is also critical to be where the market fund flows are improving for the best returns. As many long-term traders know well, it is very rare for one type of investment to lead in consecutive years. More details in my 2025 Market Forecast article explain the models and process. I am certain again this year that we will see more rotation and if you are just riding the buy-and-hold approach, you can find yourself in a painful situation.

Chart 1. The NDR chart below shows the fewest number of S&P 500 (SP500) stocks outperforming the S&P 500 index the last two years since 1998/99.

This article builds on many prior Total Return Breakout articles and emphasizes the extreme volatility this year, especially in the Technology sector:

  • Total Return Breakouts: Records, Skew, And All New Portfolios For 2025
  • 2025 Market Forecast Article.

Let me briefly introduce a sample of stocks and funds from three different portfolios. The sample selections this week come from our long-term Piotroski-Graham Value portfolio, Negative Forensic Value portfolio and Sector Gauge ETF breakout tracker. These selections represent a small sample from among 10 different portfolio models shared in our investing group at regular weekly, monthly, and annual intervals depending on the portfolio type. Next, I will explain the value selection process and the unique overlap with current momentum breakout conditions. Lastly, (if you haven't realized it already) I will share why timing matters greatly, even in our value investing portfolios for long-term growth.

  • FinVolution Group (FINV)
  • UP Fintech Holding Limited (TIGR)
  • Direxion Daily FTSE China Bull 3X Shares ETF (YINN).

Three Selections in the Positive Signal

First, We Have the FinVolution Group ADR

FinVolution comes from our Piotroski-Graham Value portfolio, intended for two-year measurement returns following the scholars’ same two-year test period. This is an exclusively fundamental finance selection based on many key value ratios considered in two different scholarly models separated by decades in their published research. The proven Buy-and-Hold returns across the full 2-year timeline are well documented, but we know well that timing matters greatly, and signals can improve all your trading.

In the long-term value portfolio, we leverage published financial algorithms from scholars like Benjamin Graham and Joseph Piotroski, who have shown strong returns in the published literature. I have combined their two algorithms and enhanced them further to release these Piotroski-Graham value portfolios two times a year, in July and January.

What is unique about FinVolution since its selection in our July mid-year Piotroski value portfolio is the high frequency of the extreme Segment 6 positive MDA breakout signals since last year. Our MDA methodology explained in a webinar here on Seeking Alpha uses fundamental, behavioral, and technical variables to signal enhanced breakout time periods.

The excellent valuations of 7.22x earnings and 0.69x sales with no debt and strong buying momentum support continue price growth to much higher levels in the coming weeks. While FINV is already up 75% from July, it still offers a good 2.9% dividend along with excellent value to retest prior highs back to at least 10/share from 2021.

Second, We Have UP Fintech Holding Limited

UP Fintech comes from the January 2025 Negative Forensic value portfolio.

I get plenty of questions about this model that selects the most negative scoring stocks using four of the top forensic algorithms in the financial literature. One of the most prominent negative forensic stocks was Enron, and for many years the company was one of the best-performing stocks in the market. In the 3 years before any irregularities were explained to be the most extreme kind of irregularities, the stock gained over 600%. Companies that have these negative scores can take steps to normalize the forensic ratios, or address the red flags that these accounting ratios represent for a wide variety of reasons well beyond the scope of this article.

Negative scores do not necessarily confirm anything bad about the stock, but highlight fundamental ratios that are well outside normal accounting ranges. Such negative extremes in the algorithms can actually reveal a positive result from unusual changes. I will not go into much detail on the algorithms here, but if that field of financial analysis interests you, then consider my forensic analysis on Infosys (INFY) or on Super Micro Computer (SMCI) regarding the recent resignation of the E&Y accounting firm:

  • Super Micro Computer And Forensic Flags To Consider After E&Y Resignation
  • US Fraud examiner says forensic algorithms show no evidence of financial wrongdoing at Infosys.

Both the Positive and Negative Forensic portfolios are released twice each year here on Seeking Alpha since 2018 with strong annual results.

A few strong characteristics of the Negative Forensic stocks are the much higher levels of volatility and extremely high frequency of delisting from mergers, acquisitions, or failure to maintain required listing standards of the exchanges. You can see the volatility in the chart above with many periods of extreme positive signals preceding considerable price movements followed then by large declines.

Sector Momentum Gauges

The sector and market gauges give us a unique way to use the MDA charts to identify the most positive sectors for large gains in various bull or bear ETF/ETN funds. A current fund to highlight is the recent China bull fund breakout of Direxion Daily FTSE China Bull 3X Shares ETF, even as rising tariffs are expected to impact China trade.

Direxion Daily FTSE China Bull 3X Shares ETF

The current breakout is crossing above a key resistance level for February as large market inflows are flooding into Chinese stocks. Part of this momentum comes from the declining value of the dollar from the January peak at the highest levels since November 2022. Lower dollar values tend to benefit financial stocks, emerging markets, and especially commodities like gold priced in dollars.

This dollar trade that correlates strongly with emerging markets and commodities like Gold is illustrated in the Basic Material sector gauge weekly chart below. The current signal is positive since mid-January and shown using (UYM) ProShares Ultra Materials in yellow. Other Basic Material managed ETFs may correlate better with the signals between the red/green momentum gauge crossovers, but Ultra Materials is also highly positive +13.55% YTD.

FINRA cautions investors that:

Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) that offer leverage or that are designed to perform inversely to the index or benchmark they track-or both-are growing in number and popularity. While such products may be useful in some sophisticated trading strategies, they are highly complex financial instruments that are typically designed to achieve their stated objectives on a daily basis. Due to the effects of compounding, their performance over longer periods of time can differ significantly from their stated daily objective. Therefore, inverse and leveraged ETFs that are reset daily typically are unsuitable for retail investors who plan to hold them for longer than one trading session, particularly in volatile markets.

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in NVDL. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

Market Outlook

While our portfolios go out in fixed weekly, monthly and semi-annual schedules in our investment group regardless of market conditions, we can still benefit from market signals. Experience has shown that fighting against the MDA market variables can be very adverse to our returns. The market really doesn’t care how good we think of our selected stocks or market valuations, and it is wise to trade accordingly.

The weekly S&P 500 gauges are still negative as we continue through Q4 earnings, with mega giants Walmart (WMT) and Nvidia (NVDA) still left to report. We are watching to see if more sectors can turn positive next week.

Conclusion

Scholars have documented for years that, “harvesting different risk baskets, each with a premium that has low-to-negative correlations to the others” is an excellent way to produce strong risk-adjusted returns. These ongoing sector rotations and signals continue to give us many new opportunities for profit in the years ahead as we build our optimal portfolio mixes each year.

As I detailed in prior articles, I am focused on a rotation to value this year that may greatly benefit out-of-favor stocks over prior momentum gainers.

The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) suggests more declines for the dollar ahead that may greatly benefit emerging markets and commodities priced in dollars. Conversely, a rising dollar tends to be worse for trade and the broader markets.

This article shares a few ways to detect strong opportunities early and leverage unique statistical models published in financial literature for our benefit. You may be able to leverage our selections and signals even better than I do.

I wish you well in all your trading decisions!

JD Henning, PhD.

References

J. Bradford DeLong, Andrei Shleifer, Lawrence Summers, and Robert Waldmann. “Noise Trader Risk in Financial Markets,” Journal of Political Economy 98 (1990). 703-738.

data.title: CVS Health: One Good Quarter Is Not Enough; Retain Sell
data.description:
Performance Assessment

In mid-Dec'24, I retained my 'Sell' view on CVS Health (NYSE:CVS). This was unfortunate as the stock has rallied hard since then:

As a result, a lot of the active return generated since my initiating coverage's 'Sell' view on CVS has been eroded away, although the stock has still lagged the S&P500 (SPY) (SPX) (IVV) (VOO) since:

Thesis

CVS Health reported Q4 FY24 results last week. Upon review, I see arguments that favor both the bulls and the bears. But overall, I am inclined to still retain a bearish outlook on the stock:

  1. Q4 results delivery suggests that the market had been too pessimistic.
  2. Q4 saw yet another quarter of margin erosion in the insurance business.
  3. But profitability pressures may ease in FY25.
  4. Transparent pricing via CostVantage may boost volumes in the pharmacy business.
  5. Valuations are at a lower than usual discount vs peers.
  6. Relative technicals outlook is still bearish amid a likely bull trap.
  7. High leverage levels are a key risk monitorable.

Q4 results delivery suggests that the market had been too pessimistic

CVS delivered a weak and less than usual revenue beat (+0.9%) vs median consensus estimates:

I think a contributing factor of the weaker than usual performance was the fact that membership in the health care benefits (insurance) portfolio started to drop in advance of 2025 rate increases:

Medical membership of approximately 27.1 million was roughly flat sequentially as membership in our Individual Exchange business started to modestly decline in advance of the 2025 rate increases we took in that book.

- CFO Thomas Cowhey in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

But the biggest and most unexpected surprise was a 35bps beat on EBIT margins:

This is meaningful when we put things in context; CVS' biggest issue in recent quarters has been declining profitability. So margin performance that beat expectations despite headwinds continuing (discussed more in the following section) suggests that the market may have been too pessimistic on the stock. I think this has been the driving factor of the +20% rally in the stock post the Q4 FY24 earnings release.

Q4 saw yet another quarter of margin erosion in the insurance business

On a TTM basis, CVS' health care benefits (insurance) business makes up 35% and 32% of company-wide revenues and gross margins, respectively. But it has a mere 2.7% of adjusted EBIT, with TTM EBIT margins of only 0.2%. Despite higher than expected profitability performance, CVS' issues in its health care benefits (insurance) business has seen no end to steady gross margin erosion:

This has been driven mostly by higher medical benefit ratios (MBR), which is a measure of health insurance business' profitability:

MBR is calculated by taking insurance costs that occur above the gross profit line and dividing that by insurance premium revenue.

Q4's rise in MBR was driven by a suite of factors:

This increase was primarily driven by higher utilization, the premium impact of lower Star ratings for payment year 2024 and higher acuity in Medicaid.

- CFO Thomas Cowhey in the Q4 FY24 earnings call.

It looks like Wall St analysts expect these pressures to continue, as they have generally downgraded EPS estimates for most of FY25:

Notwithstanding that, we should note that there are some elements in play that could ease profitability pressures this year.

But profitability pressures may ease in FY25

Firstly, CVS' insurance business will be benefiting from better insurance policy terms in 2025:

We are cautiously optimistic that our benefit design changes in 2025 will help alleviate some of the supplemental benefit pressure… During the quarter, we made additional progress on rate updates, and with over 40% of our book having repriced in early January 2025, we have a line of sight to a mid-4 percentage point rate increase. Our overall rate advocacy efforts are currently on track.

- CFO Thomas Cowhey in the Q4 FY24 earnings call.

Secondly, management expects to end FY25 with a high single-digit decline in Medicare Advantage memberships:

We also expect our Medicare Advantage membership to end the year down a high single-digit percentage from year-end 2024… reflects strong execution by our teams as they made the difficult choices necessary to improve Medicare Advantage profitability in 2025.

- CFO Thomas Cowhey in the Q4 FY24 earnings call.

This is important because Medicare Advantage members make up a decent 16.4% of overall CVS insurance members. And it is the least profitable membership group for CVS due to higher incidences of higher cost claims. From an EBIT perspective, it has been posting losses in the -4.5% to -5% range. But with changes in policy terms and an expected decline in the Medicare Advantage member counts, the management team is looking to move toward 3% to 5% EBIT margins in this business, enabled via a 100bps improvement in MBRs to 91.5%.

This is the bullish side of the story. However, I am wary of being too quick to believe this narrative as the company's execution record has been poor in FY24, with multiple cuts to its forecasts.

Transparent pricing via CostVantage may boost volumes in the pharmacy business

On a TTM basis, CVS' pharmacy business makes up 33%, 48% and 42% of revenues, gross profits and adjusted EBIT respectively. So this is another key driver of the stock's performance.

Now, CVS has a new drug pricing called CostVantage, which aims to simplify and make drug pricing more transparent based on its underlying cost, a defined markup and a dispensing fee to reflect value in the pharmacy service provided. It has been rolled out to all commercial prescriptions as of 6 Jan'25. I see this as a tailwind to prescription volumes growth in the pharmacy business:

In FY25, I expect high single-digit growth going ahead in prescription volumes filled.

Valuations are at a lower than usual discount vs peers

CVS is trading at a 1-yr fwd PE of 11.2x, which is just a 1.1% discount to the comps' median of 11.3x. At first glance, this may indicate fair valuation. However, note that CVS has usually traded at a 27.9% discount to its comps:

So a 1.1% discount is relatively much higher than average. Hence, I would say that CVS stock is trading at a relatively higher than usual premium vs its comps.

Relative technicals outlook is still bearish amid a likely bull trap

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of CVS vs SPX500

CVS vs SPX500 shows a strong incumbent downtrend that has seen 2 months of a sharp bullish correction. The ratio prices right now are right near the monthly resistance level. I note that there has been minimal sideways rollover or consolidation, which is typical in trend reversals according to Wyckoff theory. Hence, I am inclined to believe the recent move up is more likely to be a bull trap and that there is some more downside and hence underperformance vs the S&P500 to follow.

High leverage levels are a key risk monitorable

Whilst much of the focus on CVS may be on its profitability profile, I think we should also keep on eye on its leverage levels:

As profitability has been pressured, CVS' net debt/EBITDA has been steadily rising to 4.7x on a TTM basis as of the Dec'24 quarter. I think this is quite an elevated level for a business like CVS. So this is a key risk to monitor for the bulls.

Takeaway & Positioning

Since my last update on the stock in Dec'24, CVS has rallied 45%, which includes a ~20% rally after the Q4 FY24 earnings release last week. This has eroded a lot of the initial alpha generated from my first 'Sell' view on the stock in Oct'24. I think the reaction to the Q4 results may have been due to an EBIT margins beat, which is a surprise positive, even though the company continued to see margin erosion in its insurance business. I acknowledge that in FY25 there are positive catalysts for profitability improvement here, driven by more favorable policy rates for CVS' insurance business as well as reduced membership numbers from Medicare Advantage; the least profitable membership group for CVS. However, I am cautious in believing the margin expansion story right now as the company has an underwhelming track record in execution, having cut its forecasts multiple times in FY24. Another positive I acknowledge is that on the pharmacy business side, I believe the transparent CostVantage pricing model change may help boost prescription volumes.

But considering a lower than usual discount valuation vs its comps, a likely bull trap in the relative technicals vs the S&P500 and rising leverage levels, I choose to retain my 'Sell' view.

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

Historical performance for the I Class prior to its inception is based on the performance of R5 Class shares. I Class performance has been adjusted to reflect differences in expenses between classes, if applicable. Pre-inception differences in R5 Class and I Class performance are based on rounding.

Expense ratio is as of the fund's current prospectus. The I Class minimum investment amount is$5 million($3 million for endowments and foundations) per fund. The R5 Share Class is available only to participants in group employer-sponsored retirement plans where a financial intermediary provides recordkeeping services to plan participants.

Periods greater than one year have been annualized.

Market Review

Bonds retreated. Alongside rising Treasury yields and persistent inflation, U.S. bonds broadly declined in the fourth quarter. However, a strong rally in the third quarter, aided by the September launch of the Federal Reserve’s(Fed’s) latest easing campaign, helped the market maintain a year-to-date gain.

Economy remained resilient. The U.S. economy grew 3.1%(annualized) in the third quarter, similar to the second quarter. Other data released in the fourth quarter were generally upbeat. Led by services sector strength, activity in the private sector expanded, while consumer confidence improved and retail sales increased. However, manufacturing continued to contract, and the job market weakened.

Fed cut rates, altered its outlook. The Fed followed up its 0.5% September rate cut with two 0.25% cuts in the fourth quarter. This pushed the Fed’s rate target range to 4.25% to 4.5%. But amid persistent inflation, Fed officials adopted a more cautious stance, boosting their inflation and growth forecasts and reducing their rate cut outlook. They indicated future cuts would be data dependent.

Treasury yields rose. Despite a brief postelection rally, Treasury yields ended the quarter higher, and the yield curve between two and 10 years steepened. Above-target inflation, mounting U.S. debt and changing expectations regarding Fed policy contributed to the yield backdrop. The yield on the 10-year note climbed 0.79% to 4.58%, while the two-year Treasury yield increased 0.6% to 4.25%.

Inflation persisted. Inflation increased for the second straight month in November. Annual core personal consumption expenditures, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, rose to 2.8% in October and remained there in November. Inflation break-even rates increased, and Treasury inflation-protected securities(TIPS) outperformed nominal Treasuries.

All investment-grade bond sectors declined. Although credit spreads tightened in the quarter, rising yields pressured investment-grade corporate bond returns, which declined but fared slightly better than Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities(MBS). High-yield corporates advanced fractionally and outperformed their investment-grade peers.

Portfolio Performance Review

Duration and yield curve positioning detracted. Entering the quarter, the portfolio had a slightly longer-than-index duration position, which weighed on results as yields climbed in October. By early November, we reduced duration to neutral, where it remained through quarter-end. In addition, our positioning along the yield curve modestly detracted from results.

Out-of-index positions contributed. Out-of-index stakes in the securitized and corporate sectors lifted relative performance. In the securitized sector, allocations to non-agency collateralized mortgage obligations, agency MBS, non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities and asset-backed securities were top contributors. Non-index investment-grade corporates also boosted results.

Inflation exposure slightly aided results. We held an underweight position relative to the index in TIPS, which modestly lifted performance as TIPS yields increased. We also held inflation swaps in conjunction with securitized and corporate bonds to manage the portfolio’s inflation exposure. This position boosted relative results as inflation break-even rates ended the quarter higher.

Portfolio Positioning

Economy may slow before gaining momentum. We expect slower growth in the first half of 2025, as Trump administration initiatives, including new tariffs and government efficiency efforts, likely weaken consumer demand and employment. By the second half, deregulation, onshoring and rekindled animal spirits may take hold, creating renewed economic momentum.

Inflation should ease. We believe inflation should gradually move toward the 2% target, enabling additional Fed easing—probably more than the market currently expects. Meanwhile, President-elect Donald Trump’s policy priorities, including tariffs, deportations and onshoring, could push inflation higher later in the year. But a strong dollar and energy sector deregulation may temper those effects.

Security selection remains key. Our research efforts continue to help uncover value and manage risk. Trade wars, inflation fears and rate volatility could lead to better valuations and select opportunities in credit sectors. We also believe agency MBS valuations are attractive. In addition high-quality, short-duration structured credit offers strong relative value and risk/reward profiles.

Data presented reflects past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance shown. To obtain performance data current to the most recent month end, please visit www.americancentury.com/performance. Investment return and share value will fluctuate, and redemption value may be more or less than original cost. Data assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. For information about other share classes available, please consult the prospectus. There is no guarantee that the investment objectives will be met. Dividends and yields represent past performance and there is no guarantee that they will continue to be paid.

You should consider the fund’s investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses carefully before you invest. The fund’s prospectus or summary prospectus, which can be obtained at American Century Investments® Home, contains this and other information about the fund, and should be read carefully before investing.

The opinions expressed are those of the portfolio investment team and are no guarantee of the future performance of any American Century Investments portfolio. Statements regarding specific holdings represent personal views and compensation has not been received in connection with such views. This information is for an educational purpose only and is not intended to serve as investment advice.

The information is not intended as a personalized recommendation or fiduciary advice and should not be relied upon for investment, accounting, legal or tax advice.

Generally, as interest rates rise, the value of the securities held in the fund will decline. The opposite is true when interest rates decline.

The prospectus contains very important information about the characteristics of the underlying security and potential tax implications of owning this fund.

Source: Bloomberg Index Services Ltd. Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation Protected Securities(TIPS) Index consists of Treasury inflation protected securities with a remaining maturity of one year or more.

American Century Investments Services, Inc., Distributor©2025 American Century Proprietary Holdings, Inc. All rights reserved.

Non-FDIC Insured | May Lose Value | No Bank Guarantee

Commentary Glossary

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities(Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax(AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities(ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points(BPs): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for“carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations(CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations(CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities(CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index(CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration(CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or“cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest-paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures(usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency

overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets(EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund(ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee(FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve(Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts(Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation(GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product(GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises(GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield(HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade(IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard & Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A“market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships(MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher-yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI(All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into"pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures(PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing(QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts(REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool.

ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later(to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities(maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality(credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard“swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns(or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities(TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct"full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills(maturing in one year or less), notes(maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds(maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes(VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic(daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any"inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Glossary Sources: American Century Investments, Bloomberg Indices.

American Century Investments Services, Inc., Distributor©2025 American Century Proprietary Holdings, Inc. All rights reserved.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Commodity Exposure Through The PDBC ETF
data.description:
I last wrote about the Invesco Optimum Yield Diversified Commodity Strategy No K-1 ETF (NASDAQ:PDBC) product on Seeking Alpha on October 8, 2024.

PDBC is a unique product because it is exposed to futures prices. However, its structure allows the ETF to pay shareholders dividends without the K-1 tax form that many other commodity futures ETFs, like DBC, must distribute.

PDBC provides exposure to commodities, with a significant allocation to traditional energy commodities. PDBC’s nominal price action does not reflect its performance, as its dividend structure reveals the actual returns.

PDBC provides exposure to commodity prices

The fund summary for the Invesco Optimum Yield Diversified Commodity Strategy No K-1 ETF product states:

The most recent top holdings of PDBC were:

The ETF was 20.64% exposed to energy commodities, 2.62% to precious metals, 7.77% to agricultural commodities, and 5.21% to base metals. PDBC is an energy-centric commodity ETF.

At $13.63 per share, PDBC had $4.76 billion in assets under management. PDBC trades an average of over 5.95 million shares daily and charges around a 0.60% management fee.

A slight loss in 2024 - Dividend payouts made PDBC profitable last year

A composite of the 29 leading commodities on U.S. and U.K. exchanges rose 12.5% in 2024, while the energy sector was 4.48% higher.

The chart shows PDBC’s 2.33% decline in 2024, moving from $13.30 at the end of 2023 to $12.99 per share at the end of 2024. However, PDBC paid shareholders a 57-cent annual dividend in late December, translating to a 4.3% yield. The net result was around a 2% 2024 gain.

A rally in early 2025

After closing 2024 at $12.99 per share, PDBC moved higher in early 2025.

At the $13.63 level, PDBC is 4.93% higher over the first two months of 2025. Meanwhile, the dividend yield is a function of commodities asset class returns.

Passing Seeking Alpha ETF grades

PDBC receives across-the-board passing Seeking Alpha ETF Grades.

The A+ in liquidity reflects the $476 billion in assets and over 5.95 million daily trading volumes. The C+ in dividends is a function of the annual payout, which makes PDBC a no K-1 product. The share action and risk in the volatile commodities asset class earn the ETF a D+ in momentum and a C- in risk. The 0.60% management fee results in a C in expenses.

PDBC is a passive investment vehicle that adds commodity exposure to portfolios. Shareholders do not have to worry about filing K-1 reports with annual taxes as the returns are in dividends.

The case for and against commodities in 2025

The case for commodities exposure in early 2025 includes:

  • Inflation remains elevated above the Fed’s 2% target level. Higher inflation supports rising commodity prices.
  • The trend in the commodities asset class has been higher since the 2020 pandemic-inspired low. Market trends are always a trader’s and investor’s best friend.
  • Tariffs and geopolitical tensions tend to cause volatility in commodity markets. While production is localized, consumption is ubiquitous.
  • The demand for alternative asset investments supports capital flow into the commodities asset class.
  • Gold, coffee, cocoa, and frozen concentrated orange juice have made new record highs over the past weeks and months, and many other commodities have also reached record or multi-year highs since 2020.

The case for caution in commodities includes:

  • High interest rates for longer increase the cost of carrying commodity inventories, weighing on the demand.
  • Higher U.S. interest rates tend to push the U.S. dollar higher compared to other currencies. A rising dollar is typically bearish for raw material prices.
  • The Trump administration has pledged to lower crude oil and oil product prices in a two-pronged approach: increasing U.S. oil and gas output and pressuring Saudi Arabia and OPEC to increase production. Lower oil prices reduce inflationary pressures, which weigh on commodity prices as energy is a critical component of all goods and services.
  • Commodities are highly volatile assets that experience boom and bust price action.

I remain bullish on commodity prices in 2025. Still, buying on price weakness will likely be optimal as the volatile asset class will experience peaks and valleys on price charts. Buying during corrections has been the most successful approach since the 2020 lows, and I expect that to continue in 2025. PDBC is a highly liquid product that adds commodity exposure to diversified portfolios without the bother of K-1 reports.

data.title: TMSC And Intel: Just The Beginning Of A New Onshoring Reality
data.description:
In early January, I wrote a piece about Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), which said that, onshoring chip fabrication in Europe and the US will turn Intel from a dog to a god.

Not everyone laughed. The word is out; chip production must come onshore and fast, and Intel is the major installed Fab in the US and Europe.

Consequentially, this week, Intel went through the roof. As I write, my January 8th tip is up 18% - not a bad call for a clueless crypto Brit.

Why? For exactly the reasons I suggested.

The US cannot afford to have its critical technology made by a self-described adversary or by an ally a short steam from that adversary. Put another way, where is the Nasdaq trading the day after the Taiwan Strait gets spicy?

Last week, the US threw Europe under the bus.

‘Get your defense sorted out; we have our plate full in the Pacific’ was the message.

If you can’t believe that, go and watch Vance and Hegseth on YouTube; the reactions from the EU elite were priceless as the message was sounded loud and clear. The whole position of technical dominance evaporates if you can’t make the chips that drive everything in your economy and infrastructure. While it seemed smart to send that tech to be made cheaply in Asia, it seems extremely dumb when you have to project ‘hard power’ there, and the core of that projection is made by the folks you are trying to bully.

So, my speculation that the US government encouraging onshoring with $30bn of incentives will morph into forcing an acceleration of onshoring has now hit the mainstream, and, as a result, Intel has shot up.

Whether Intel is going to JV with TMSC doesn’t actually matter; it’s the long-term necessity to do such deals and to build out chip fabrication in the US and Europe that puts Intel in a prime spot to leverage its comeback in this new reality.

DeepSeek is another warning to the US that time is up to make a move. There is no second place in AI. The winner takes all, so you cannot have your AI chips made outside of your security zone. DeepSeek has apparently sucked the AI brains out of ChatGPT using what is called ‘distillation.’ That slap in the face crystalizes the fact that companies must secure their tech or else it is gone. When it is gone, with it goes the ability to defend their economies and sovereignty.

I hold Intel and GlobalFoundries because these assets have market caps that are peanuts compared to the likes of Nvidia, Apple and other giga-cap companies that would implode if they couldn’t get their chips made. As the tide of free trade and comparative advantage economics recede, established onshore players will flourish as globalization goes into reverse. Consequentially, Intel at $50 a share doesn’t seem that wild a target.

data.title: Vulcan Materials Company 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Vulcan Materials Company in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: After Sales Rebound In January, AVITA Is Set Up For A Monster Year
data.description:
Q4 In The Rear View Mirror

It’ll be pretty easy to forget the sequential sales decline in Q4 2024 if 2025 shows the consisted sequential sales growth shareholders have been hoping for. As for the February call, we got a slightly more detailed explanation of what happened in Q4 and early green shoots on Q1 2025. Specifically, management stated a few of its biggest accounts didn’t finalize pending purchases in December in order to preserve cash. This hurt sales by $3-$4 million. In January, sales normalized which should lead to a strong Q1.

AVITA’s (NASDAQ:RCEL) $18.4 million in Q4 sales missed guidance by between $3.9 million and $5.9 million. This implies most of the miss was due to the sales deferrals. At the midpoints, it leaves a $1.4 million shortfall related to fewer acute wounds in the quarter than expected. If sales had missed estimates, but still showed sequential growth, the stock’s reaction wouldn’t have been nearly as bad. The stock isn’t expensive enough to crater on a slight miss. However, the sequential decline made investors fear an adoption reversal. This ended up not happening as we can see from January’s rebound.

The stock also fell due to balance sheet worries and the OrbiMed debt deal. I’ll detail the exact changes to the OrbiMed deal later on, but I’ll mention here the trailing 12-month sales minimum in Q1 is $73 million. These new sales minimums aren’t guidance in the sense they aren’t what the firm expects to hit. However, since the minimums are meant to be sales marks the firm can comfortably hit, they do tell us what management expects. Specifically, $73 million in TTM (trailing 12-month) sales implies $19.84 million in Q1 sales. If that’ll be comfortably hit, it supports my previous projection for $21 million. In fact, results might even be slightly better than that since management said sales would be up notably from Q4.

We could easily see $22 million in sales which would flow nicely through to the bottom line since gross margins are 87.6%. I will raise my Q1 estimate to $21.5 million for accuracy’s sake. Management mentioned at the JP Morgan conference that it saw a slight benefit from the Louisiana terrorist attack and the California wildfires. I won’t add these events to my estimate, since I’m not going to review every single acute event in America every quarter. Just remember, this is an event-driven business. In a long timeframe, they are predictable. In a short timeframe, anything can happen.

With the January rebound, the weak Q4 has been put in the rearview mirror. I’m sure investors will want to see a few quarters of growth before trusting the firm completely. That starts with one good report which we will get in May. Management mentioned it has been doing 1k burn cases per month in the past 1-2 quarters and growing. With RECELL GO cartridges priced at $6.5k, that’s $19.5 million in sales per quarter. That’s not including the full-thickness wound indication. I don’t want to read too much into that comment, but at minimum it suggests the firm is at a quarterly sales run rate in the low $20s (millions).

Net Cash Used In Operations Increased As Expected

Before I get into the cash usage in the quarter, I must mention management bungled its communication on its breakeven guidance timing in its January update. Investors had been anxiously waiting for the firm to hit breakeven for a few quarters. Management pushing the target back from Q3 to Q4 hurt investor confidence. Now management is suggesting it can get to FCF positive in 2H and GAAP profitability in Q4.

There was no need to push back guidance if they think they can reach FCF positivity in Q3, since likely no investors are worried about GAAP profitability. Rather, investors are worried about the firm having enough cash to get to breakeven. The stock cratered in January because investors were confused when management said the Q4 sales decline was a one-time event, yet still pushed back the breakeven target. Personally, I loved the stock’s decline because it gave me my first chance to buy shares since I started following the company a couple of years ago.

I’ll go over management’s framing of the firm’s loss in Q4, but I must first stick with how I analyzed it last quarter to stay consistent. I can’t move from benchmark to benchmark to fit my preconceived narrative. As you can see below, AVITA’s net cash used in operations increased from $7.2 million to $8.1 million. That was around what I expected because sales fell $1.14 million sequentially. If sales increase from $18.4 million to $21.5 million in Q1, cash usage can decline to $6 million. At year end, the firm had $35.9 million in cash. In a future section, I’ll review my cash burn projections. Not to spoil things, but I think the firm can get to breakeven without needing to raise money.

Management framed the loss using different stats. Neither my analysis nor this framing are incorrect. They are just different ways of viewing the business. Let’s look at what management discussed. In Q4, AVITA used $8.5 million in cash which was down from $9.7 million in the previous quarter. This was due to the $4.1 million decline in sequential operating expenses.

On a year over year basis, operating expenses increased from $24.7 million to $26.1 million. There was a $3.9 million increase in sales and marketing expenses. This was offset by a $600k decrease in G&A and a $1.9 million decrease in R&D due to the TONE study completion. I expect sales & marketing as well as G&A expenses to remain stable in the next few quarters because the firm isn’t increasing its headcount. The ending of the TONE study is a nice tailwind, but R&D expenses from the Cohealyx 1 study will cancel that out. The firm won’t reach breakeven via cutting costs. Rather, it will gain leverage via high gross margin sales while maintaining a steady headcount.

The firm faced a cash headwind in Q4 due to accounts receivable increasing from $7.7 million last year to $11.8 million. I don’t like how management framed the sequential improvement in cash usage in relation to the year over year increase in accounts receivable. It’s better to compare sequential numbers and annual numbers separately. Accounts receivable increased from $10.3 million in Q3. That’s a valid headwind to point out since that’s a 14.6% increase in spite of a 5.8% sequential sales decline.

RECELL GO Transition & RECELL GO Mini Launch

AVITA is trying to quickly transition center usage from RECELL to RECELL GO because RECELL GO’s ease of use will lower hospitals’ burden and increase usage. RECELL GO has 1/3rd fewer steps, streamlining preparation. The goal to transition +95% of centers to RECELL GO by year-end was slightly missed. AVITA transitioned nearly all of its burn accounts to RECELL GO and transitioned over 70% of trauma centers treating full-thickness wounds. That equates to 83% of total unit volume. The transition will probably be complete in Q1. Management didn’t mention this, but I speculate some trauma centers will go directly from RECELL to RECELL GO Mini. There’s no need to use RECELL GO first. Given the need for RECELL GO Mini, I see nothing hampering the completion of the transition.

Since I follow AVITA closer than most investors, I’d like to share a confusing point I figured out along the way. The slide below shows the 398,500 RECELL-eligible procedures within the full-thickness wound market. AVITA’s management is first going after acute wounds even though RECELL GO can treat chronic wounds. This makes sense because treating a chronic wound without fixing its underlying cause won’t solve the issue permanently like it will for a wound from a traumatic event or a surgery.

Therefore, I boxed off the acute full-thickness wounds AVITA will be going after first. This confusion was spurred by me trying to forecast the potential uptick from RECELL GO Mini. This device will help AVITA go after wounds less than 2.5% of total body surface area. These small wounds represent the vast majority of the 270k non-chronic full-thickness wounds AVITA is pushing to treat.

RECELL GO Mini launched in February after the firm completed training preparations. It’s difficult to project the device’s cartridge sales in Q1 since the quarter only includes the first few weeks of uptake. Management had little sales information to go by as of the February 13th call. Mini uses the same processing device as RECELL GO. The 3 wells for the skin, buffer, and enzyme are just smaller.

Even if we knew the exact percentage of the 270k full-thickness wounds that are 2.5% or smaller, we wouldn’t know the number of doctors who were hesitant to use RECELL GO because it treats up to 10% of total body surface area. The only observations I can make are the full-thickness wound market is 1.33x the size of the burn market and the full-thickness launch has gone through fits and starts. If RECELL GO Mini effectively eases doctor’s concerns, AVITA will have accelerating revenue growth in 2025. I look forward to management breaking down sales between burns and full-thickness wounds.

Cohealyx 1 Starting In March, Early Signs Are Amazing

Personally, I am driven to invest in medical stocks due to treatment improvements versus the current standard of care. Science leads financial results. You can’t predict where the business is headed by looking at the 3 major financial statements. You must look at study data and the early experiences doctors and patients have with the devices.

This brings me to the dermal matrix called Cohealyx. It received FDA approval in December based on porcine models. AVITA went through 18 creations to settle on this dermal matrix. It has the right histological absorption so it doesn’t need to be removed, yet provides the structure for tissue and blood vessel re-growth. In full-thickness wounds, the body lacks the cells to repopulate connective tissue which leaves the wound open. The longer the wound stays open, the greater the risk of infection and scarring.

In looking at the stages of wound care, PermeaDerm can be used in the beginning and the end. In the beginning, PermeaDerm temporizes the wound. It can also be used as a dressing after RECELL is applied. PermeaDerm can be used 3-4 times in total in the wound treatment process. Getting back to Cohealyx, the dermal matrix is used to get the wound graft ready. Cohealyx re-grows connective tissue to allow the split thickness graft to take. It can be used with or without RECELL GO, increasing the addressable market.

AVITA needs IRB (Institutional Review Board) approval before it can start Cohealyx’s post-approval human study. The goal is to enroll 40 patients in 20 centers (study called Cohealyx 1). There will be a 26-week measurement period, but AVITA doesn’t need to wait that long to use the data in Cohealyx’ commercialization. The study was designed so AVITA can use the data as it comes out. Since Cohealyx can get wounds graft ready in just 7 days, it’ll have enough complete responses to get centers to try it quickly after the study starts. The goal is to commence commercialization on April 1st.

Cohealyx has been used on a few patients prior to formal enrollment of Cohealyx 1. The images below are from the first patient treated at The Ohio State Wexner Medical Center. The wound was graft ready after 7 days just like in the porcine models. None of the pre-study wounds have taken longer than 7 days to be graft ready. According to the article posted on The Ohio State website, the current standard of care gets wounds graft ready after 2-4 weeks. The first patient wasn’t treated with RECELL after Cohealyx (study enrolls patients with and without RECELL). Just by using Cohealyx, the patient’s hospital stay was cut from what would have been 1 month, to just 10 days. Cohealyx’s ease of use in the operating room could allow more patients to be treated.

Management showed Cohealyx to 50 physicians and they all wanted to try it. It’s easy to understand why because the potential advantages are straightforward. To spur adoption, AVITA will price the dermal matrix at a 20% discount to the competition. The TAM calculations management has shown include this discounted price. Besides improved time to graft and a lower price, AVITA will sell Cohealyx via a consignment model with RFID tracking for better inventory management. Consignment is when the sale only occurs when the dermal matrix is used. AVITA will store Cohealyx at the centers. With all these advantages, I expect Cohealyx to get through Value Analysis Committees (VACs) quickly. Cohealyx’s attach rate to RECELL GO should increase rapidly over the next few quarters.

My New Sales Estimate & Valuation Target

As I mentioned, I think $21.5 million in sales in Q1 is reasonable. Management mentioned PermeaDerm is finally gaining momentum and RECELL GO sales rebounded after the weak Q4. It’s difficult to model much sales from RECELL GO Mini in Q1 since it launched in February. In Q2, I foresee $24.5 million in sales due to expanded usage of RECELL GO Mini in the full-thickness wound indication and early usage of Cohealyx. I expect a bigger increase in Cohealyx usage starting in 2H which is why I project $29.5 million and $31.5 million in sales in Q3 and Q4.

If you compare this to my last estimate, I shifted sales slightly from Q2 to Q3 to allow Cohealyx more time to gain adoption. I became more optimistic on 2nd half sales due to my increased confidence in Cohealyx. This equates to $107 million in 2025 sales which is slightly above management’s guidance high-end of $106 million and above my previous estimate of $103.5 million.

I will detail how various PermeaDerm/Cohealyx attach rates can impact sales in the next few quarters. As I mentioned, 1,000 burns treated per month equates to $19.5 million in RECELL cartridge sales. A 100% attach rate at the low-end of the listed prices of PermeaDerm and Cohealyx equates to $6 million and $60 million in sales per quarter. Remember, this doesn’t include full-thickness wounds which can be treated with all products. Furthermore, PermeaDerm can be used 3-4 times per wound and Cohealyx can be used without RECELL GO. Also, burns treated per month will increase.

A 10% attach rate equates to $600k and $6 million in sales per quarter for PermeaDerm and Cohealyx. If in 2025 the firm gets both to a 10% attach rate, burn treatments increase, and RECELL GO Mini increases market share in full-thickness wounds, my estimate will be too low.

If AVITA trades at 10x sales, that equates to a $1.07 billion market cap. As I will get to next, AVITA issued warrants to adjust the OrbiMed debt deal. The firm ended 2024 with 26,146,234 shares outstanding. I’ll conservatively estimate the firm will end 2025 with 27 million shares outstanding. This gives me a price target of $39.63. The stock is below my price target because of worries about RECELL GO adoption due to the 2 sales misses in 2024; the lack of proven adoption of its new products including RECELL GO Mini, PermeaDerm, and Cohealyx; and worries about the cash burn/balance sheet which I’ll review next.

OrbiMed Deal Amended Again, Cash Burn Estimate

For the 4th time, AVITA amended its credit agreement with OrbiMed. In exchange for lowering the sales thresholds it needs to hit, AVITA issued warrants to OrbiMed in which it can purchase the equivalent of 145,180 shares of stock. Besides the Q1 threshold of $73 million, the firm needs to hit TTM sales of $78 million, $84 million, $92 million, and $103 million in the following 4 quarters. Each quarter subsequently needs to have TTM sales of $115 million. As you can see, the firm needs to have $92 million in sales this year which is decently below the current guidance range of $100-$106 million.

My cash burn prediction per quarter no longer includes the $2 million in quarterly repayments to OrbiMed because AVITA no longer will violate the sales minimums that would cause it to repay the $40 million loan. I tweaked my cash burn projections due to changes in my sales estimates. I think the firm will only burn another $10 million before getting to breakeven. This means its $35.9 million in cash is more than enough to get there. Once the market realizes this, the stock will rise. I was able to add to my investment position after the close on Thursday before the stock increased because I believe few investors are paying as close attention to AVITA as I am.

No Details On Trauma Centers VACs On The Call, CE Mark Delay

Management didn’t detail the number of VAC acceptances in the full-thickness wound indication for RECELL GO like they typically have in recent quarters. Management forecasted this change on the previous call. While I’m fine with this, I wish new information was added on adoption rates. Each center doesn’t do the same treatment volume, so telling investors about individual additions doesn’t give us much color on the firm’s near-term growth rate.

If the firm gives new details on its February 18th webinar, I’ll mention it in the comment section. Usually there isn’t new information in them, but they give color on the facts already provided. Just because the VAC numbers weren’t disclosed doesn’t mean approvals aren’t a risk to the future growth cadence. It's important to be cautious about the timing of future approvals given the history of delays. I think VACs will be easier to achieve with PermeaDerm and Cohealyx because they don’t define a new category of devices like RECELL does.

Management now expects RECELL GO to get the CE Mark in the middle of 2025. That’s 3 months later than they said on the last call. It has been pushed back a few times. Good thing management didn’t expect much sales from Europe, Australia, and New Zealand in 2025. I’m not surprised it has taken so long because Stereotaxis’ (STXS) MAGiC CE Mark took 8 months longer than I originally expected. America will be the primary focus for AVITA in the next few years anyway.

One final new risk is the business is more event-driven than expected. While we have a good idea of how many burns and full-thickness wounds there will be each year, occasionally some quarters have more or less than expected given the nature of acute wound events. Investors will stress less about minor sales hiccups once the firm hits breakeven.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ABN AMRO: Still Cheap At 7x Earnings; Possible 10% Total Yield
data.description:
Dutch bank ABN AMRO (OTCPK:AAVMY) (OTCPK:ABMRF) closed out 2024 on a positive note, with fourth quarter results coming in stronger than analysts expected.

Having risen around 8% on the day, ABN AMRO is now trading at a five-year high in its underlying currency, albeit the dollar-denominated ADSs are a bit lower due to FX. This has also powered a decent performance since my last update in November, with ADSs returning around 11% in that time.

Since I first covered it, ABN AMRO has basically been a classic value stock. Specifically, its steep discount to tangible book value, reasonable return on tangible equity, and ability to distribute most of its earnings to shareholders has supported an ongoing double-digit shareholder yield. While timing on capital returns has been a slight issue recently, this hasn't really altered the underlying investment case.

Looking ahead, FY2025 guidance could support a slightly better year than pre-results consensus had penciled in – with the latter already mapping to an attractive-looking P/E of ~7x and a shareholder yield of ~10%. Although I am lowering the stock a notch due to its performance since my last update, ABN AMRO continues to look cheap, and I attach a 'Buy' rating to the shares.

Solid Q4 Results & Guidance

For its fourth quarter, ABN AMRO reported pre-tax profit of €619 million, beating consensus by just over 3%. Annualizing the fourth quarter result wouldn't be particularly useful due to seasonal effects. However, full-year net income attributable to ordinary shareholders was €2.25 billion – better than the ~€2 billion I thought the bank would earn a year ago. This mapped to a return on tangible equity of roughly 10%.

The fourth quarter beat was arguably stronger than the headline figure looks. Positively, revenue of €2.24 billion was around 3% ahead of consensus. Breaking this down, net interest income of €1.67 billion beat consensus by over 4%, while fee and commission income also came in stronger than expected, with the €500 million print beating consensus by almost €30 million. This was driven by higher mortgage advisory fees as the Dutch mortgage market continued its recovery and by increased transaction volumes in the bank's clearing business.

Costs were a bit more mixed. Operating expenses of €1.61 billion missed by nearly 9%, though again this includes one-off items. Underlying operating expenses were around €1.50 billion, missing by less than 1%.

Credit costs were a circa €80 million beat. Although this is a volatile line (and therefore hard to forecast quarter-to-quarter), resilient asset quality has been an ongoing theme among the European banks I have covered this quarter, so I view this as a positive, especially given upbeat guidance (more on that below).

Looking ahead, I think the FY2025 outlook looks reasonably healthy as well. Starting with revenue, management expects the bank to generate roughly €6.3 billion in NII this year, giving a range of €6.2 billion to €6.4 billion. This would be around 3% below the FY2024 print (€6.5 billion) and around 6% below the fourth quarter run rate, with management expecting a modest headwind from the bank's interest rate hedging program.

Importantly, FY2025 guidance would still leave NII around 16% above 2022 and 22% above 2021, meaning the bank will retain most of the tailwind it received from normalizing interest rates.

Breaking things down a little further, management expects higher volumes to be offset by lower lending margins – as lower LTV ratios in Dutch mortgages slightly compresses the credit risk premium on loan yields. Positively, the Dutch mortgage market continues to rebound, with house prices and transaction volumes both up nicely last year, and management expects this to continue in FY2025. This is important as domestic mortgages account for around 60% of ABN AMRO's total loan book.

The lower-end of NII guidance assumes no change to customer deposit rates, which is probably conservative given that interest rates are falling in the Eurozone. Deposit trends looked positive at the end of last year, with the bank seeing a mix shift from term balances back into demand and current accounts.

Moreover, guidance also excludes the acquisition of German private bank Hauck Aufhäuser Lampe ('HAL'). Assuming that deal closes as planned in H1, this will contribute a small amount to full-year NII. Weighing all that up, I think there is probably more upside than downside compared to where FY2025 consensus was before results (~€6.32 billion).

I would say the same with respect to fee & commission income. Compared to pre-results FY2024 consensus of €1.88 billion, analysts saw this line rising by around 4% to €1.96 billion. Not only did ABN AMRO beat FY2024 estimates on this line by just over €20 million, but management also expects FY2025 fee income growth to be "above ambition" relative to its 3%-5% medium-term target CAGR. HAL will contribute, while the outlook for mortgage advisory fees and the bank's wealth and asset services businesses all seem positive.

Costs are arguably a more speculative source of upside. Management's outlook for flat operating expenses (~€5.3 billion) would be better than consensus. Although there are levers to pull here (some projects are winding down and external employee count is falling), operating costs have generally been a soft spot for the bank – so I wouldn't be inclined to take this at face value.

On the flip side, credit loss provisioning has been more fruitful recently. Management sees that continuing this year, with FY2025 impairment charges once again seen landing below the bank's 15-20bps through-the-cycle yardstick. Compared to pre-results consensus of 16bps, this could be a source of support for the bottom line, as was the case in recent years. I would note that ABN AMRO's domestic mortgage book has a sub-55% blended LTV, giving it plenty of scope to avoid losses.

Shares At 7x Forward P/E; Potential 10% Total Yield

While I think the above might lead to some upside to FY2025 earnings, these shares still look cheap either way, with the current €16.95 share price (~$17.70 per ADS) equating to 6.2x FY2024 EPS (€2.72 per share), 7.1x pre-results FY2025 consensus EPS (€2.39 per share), and ~0.63x year-end tangible book value per share (€26.86 per share).

Given ABN AMRO aims to pay out 50% of net income via dividends, this also implies a yield of ~8% based on the FY2024 DPS and ~7% based on the prospective FY2025 payout.

In recent years, the bank has supplemented this with €500 million in annual share buybacks, which has usually been confirmed following fourth quarter results. I mentioned last time that this year's announcement has been delayed to Q2 FY2025, as the bank was still seeking clarity on the impact of Basel IV among other issues.

There is still nothing to suggest that this is anything other than a timing problem. Earnings remain relatively robust, and given the bank's regulatory capital ratio remains a little above management's target, there could even be some upside to that figure.

Taking the prospective dividend allocation (~€970 million), buybacks (~€500 million) and the bank's current market-cap (~€14.1 billion), the total yield in respect of FY2025 would be just over 10%.

Conclusion

ABN AMRO ended 2024 with a pretty upbeat set of fourth quarter figures, beating consensus. Looking ahead, while the benefits of higher interest rates have now played out, guidance surrounding fee income and costs can still support robust FY2025 earnings. Based on the current stock price, that could set investors up for a ~10% forward shareholder yield on a single-digit P/E ratio. Accordingly, I remain bullish on ABN AMRO, rating its shares a 'Buy'.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
